html:
[403]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 秋闱不售 - 秋闱指秋季的科举考试，不售意味着考试失败。
  • 自佗处归 - 佗处指其他地方，归指返回。
  • 夜泊舟村落间 - 夜泊指夜间停船，村落间指在村庄之间。
  • 有楼翼然 - 翼然形容楼阁像翅膀一样展开。
  • 碧火如环，旋转不定 - 碧火指绿色火焰，旋转不定形容其不稳定。
  • 异而呼舟人讯之 - 异指奇怪，呼舟人讯之指叫船夫询问。
  • 慎勿声 - 慎勿声指小心不要发出声音。
  • 鬼为人所觉，且移祸于人 - 鬼为人所觉指鬼被发现，移祸于人指将灾祸转移到别人身上。
  • 见人死而不救，非夫也 - 见人死而不救指看到有人死亡却不救助，非夫也指这不是一个男人应有的行为。
  • 叩门大呼 - 叩门大呼指敲门大声呼喊。
  • 告以故 - 告以故指告诉原因。
  • 大惊 - 大惊指非常惊讶。
  • 姑妇方勃溪 - 姑妇指婆媳，勃溪指争吵。
  • 泣涕登楼 - 泣涕指哭泣，登楼指上楼。
  • 排闼而入 - 排闼指推门，入指进入。
  • 手持带立床前 - 手持带指拿着绳子，立床前指站在床前。
  • 神已痴矣 - 神已痴矣指精神恍惚。
  • 呼之逾时始觉 - 呼之逾时始觉指呼唤她很长时间才意识到。
  • 举家共劝慰之 - 举家共劝慰之指全家一起安慰她。
  • 次日抵家 - 次日抵家指第二天回到家。
  • 梦一老人谓之曰 - 梦一老人谓之曰指梦见一位老人对他说。
  • 勇于为善 - 勇于为善指勇敢行善。
  • 食其报 - 食其报指享受善行的回报。
  • 必欲食报者 - 必欲食报者指想要享受回报的人。
  • 他不敢望 - 他不敢望指他不敢奢望。
  • 问我于科名何如 - 问我于科名何如指问我科举考试的情况。
  • 笑而示以掌 - 笑而示以掌指笑着展示手掌。
  • 寤而叹曰 - 寤而叹曰指醒来后叹息说。
  • 科名无望矣 - 科名无望矣指科举考试没有希望了。
  • 其明年，竟登贤书 - 其明年，竟登贤书指第二年竟然中了举。
  • 是科主试者为何公 - 是科主试者为何公指这次考试的主考官是何公。
  • 始悟梦语之巧合也 - 始悟梦语之巧合也指开始明白梦中的预言是巧合。

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 桐乡 - 桐乡是中国的一个地方，可能指特定的地区或城市。
  • 周铁崖士钢 - 周铁崖士钢是历史人物，可能是来自桐乡的学者或官员。
  • 博士弟子员 - 博士弟子员指在儒家书院学习的学生或官员。
  • 秋闱 - 秋闱指秋季的科举考试。
  • 碧火如环，旋转不定 - 碧火如环，旋转不定指一种超自然现象，常与鬼魂或精灵相关联。
  • 鬼为人所觉，且移祸于人 - 鬼为人所觉，且移祸于人指如果鬼魂被发现，它会将灾祸转移到别人身上。
  • 见人死而不救，非夫也 - 见人死而不救，非夫也指看到有人死亡却不救助，这不是一个男人应有的行为。
  • 叩门大呼 - 叩门大呼指敲门大声呼喊。
  • 告以故 - 告以故指告诉原因。
  • 大惊 - 大惊指非常惊讶。
  • 姑妇方勃溪 - 姑妇方勃溪指婆媳之间的争吵。
  • 泣涕登楼 - 泣涕登楼指哭泣并上楼。
  • 排闼而入 - 排闼而入指推门进入。
  • 手持带立床前 - 手持带立床前指拿着绳子站在床前。
  • 神已痴矣 - 神已痴矣指精神恍惚。
  • 呼之逾时始觉 - 呼之逾时始觉指呼唤她很长时间才意识到。
  • 举家共劝慰之 - 举家共劝慰之指全家一起安慰她。
  • 次日抵家 - 次日抵家指第二天回到家。
  • 梦一老人谓之曰 - 梦一老人谓之曰指梦见一位老人对他说。
  • 勇于为善 - 勇于为善指勇敢行善。
  • 食其报 - 食其报指享受善行的回报。
  • 必欲食报者 - 必欲食报者指想要享受回报的人。
  • 他不敢望 - 他不敢望指他不敢奢望。
  • 问我于科名何如 - 问我于科名何如指问我科举考试的情况。
  • 笑而示以掌 - 笑而示以掌指笑着展示手掌。
  • 寤而叹曰 - 寤而叹曰指醒来后叹息说。
  • 科名无望矣 - 科名无望矣指科举考试没有希望了。
  • 其明年，竟登贤书 - 其明年，竟登贤书指第二年竟然中了举。
  • 是科主试者为何公 - 是科主试者为何公指这次考试的主考官是何公。
  • 始悟梦语之巧合也 - 始悟梦语之巧合也指开始明白梦中的预言是巧合。

[404]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 同治乙丑年 - Year designation in the Chinese lunar calendar, corresponding to 1865 in the Gregorian calendar.
  • 浙江補行鄉試 - Zhejiang province held a supplementary provincial examination, indicating a special examination conducted in that year.
  • 夢至一廟 - Dreamed of arriving at a temple, referring to the dreamer's experience.
  • 廟中有神南面坐 - There was a deity seated facing south in the temple, describing the deity's position.
  • 其旁胥吏甚夥 - There were many clerks and officials around, indicating the presence of numerous assistants.
  • 各負一牌，呈神坐前 - Each carrying a tablet, presenting it before the deity, describing the clerks' actions.
  • 神執筆於牌上點之 - The deity wrote on the tablet, indicating the deity's action.
  • 乃各負之出 - Then each carried it out, describing the clerks' subsequent actions.
  • 此何牌也 - What is this tablet? Asking about the content of the tablet.
  • 牌上姓名，皆今年鄉試中式人也 - The names on the tablet are all those who passed the provincial examination this year, explaining the content of the tablet.
  • 皆其人所行陰德也 - They are all the good deeds done secretly by those people, explaining the small print on the tablet.
  • 寤而訪之 - Awoke and went to find out, describing the dreamer's actions upon waking.
  • 邑中諸生果有裘治成其人者 - There indeed was a person named Qiu Zhicheng among the scholars in the county, confirming the existence of the person.
  • 往告以夢 - Went to tell him about the dream, describing the dreamer's actions.
  • 吾不應試十余年矣 - I have not taken the examination for over ten years, indicating the person's long absence from the exams.
  • 精力衰颓，学问荒落 - My energy and spirit have declined, and my learning has become neglected, describing the person's current state.
  • 自分此生永谢场屋 - I believe this life will never see me in the examination hall again, expressing the person's resignation.
  • 恐不能践君幻梦也 - I fear I cannot fulfill your dream, expressing doubt about the dream's fulfillment.
  • 遍告裘之姻族 - Told all of Qiu's relatives and friends, describing the dreamer's actions.
  • 咸纵臾之 - They all encouraged him, describing the encouragement received.
  • 乃赴试，竟举于乡 - He then took the examination and was successful, describing the outcome.
  • 或问裘有何阴德 - Someone asked Qiu what good deeds he had done, asking about the person's good deeds.
  • 前年族人有一婢，将置之死，吾力救而免之，且为择婿而嫁焉 - Last year, a servant in my clan was about to be put to death, and I saved her and arranged for her marriage, describing a specific good deed.
  • 盖无心之善，作者犹不自觉，而鬼神则已纪录之也 - It is said that unintentional good deeds, even if the doer is unaware, are recorded by the gods, explaining the significance of the good deed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 同治 - The era name of Emperor Tongzhi, who reigned from 1861 to 1875.
  • 浙江 - The name of a province in eastern China.
  • 鄉試 - A type of imperial examination held in provincial capitals.
  • 嵊县 - A county in Zhejiang province.
  • 庙 - A place of worship, often associated with deities and rituals.
  • 神 - A deity or god.
  • 胥吏 - Clerks or officials, often involved in administrative tasks.
  • 牌 - A tablet or plaque, often used in religious or official contexts.
  • 阴德 - Secret or unpublicized good deeds, often believed to have spiritual merit.
  • 邑中诸生 - Scholars or students in the county.
  • 场屋 - Examination halls or venues where imperial examinations were held.

Proper Nouns

  • 同治乙丑年 - The year 1865.
  • 浙江 - Zhejiang province.
  • 嵊县 - A county in Zhejiang province.
  • 裘治成 - A person mentioned in the story.
  • 同治 - The era name of Emperor Tongzhi.
  • 庙 - A place of worship.
  • 神 - A deity or god.
  • 胥吏 - Clerks or officials.
  • 牌 - A tablet or plaque.
  • 阴德 - Secret or unpublicized good deeds.
  • 邑中诸生 - Scholars or students in the county.
  • 场屋 - Examination halls or venues.

[405]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 工为制举文 - Skilled in composing essays for the imperial examinations.
  • 文思泉涌 - Ideas flowed like a spring.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 会稽王 - A title referring to a prince or nobleman.
  • 制举文 - Essays written for the imperial examinations, a crucial part of the Chinese civil service system.
  • 场屋 - Examination halls where the imperial examinations took place.
  • 白洋媪婆 - A midwife from Baiyang, a place known for skilled midwives.
  • 洋钱 - Foreign currency, often used in trade and as a form of payment.

Proper Nouns

  • 会稽王 - A title.
  • 湘舟济泰 - The personal name of the subject.
  • 白洋媪婆 - A midwife from Baiyang.
  • 洋钱 - Foreign currency.

[406]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不售 - Means 'did not pass' or 'was not successful.'
  • 善治生 - Means 'skilled in managing one's livelihood' or 'good at making a living.'
  • 始心动 - Means 'he began to be moved.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 山阴 - Refers to a historical region in Zhejiang, known for its cultural significance.
  • 童子试 - Refers to the preliminary examination for young scholars, a part of the imperial examination system.
  • 同治癸酉岁 - Refers to the year 1873 during the Tongzhi era, a period of restoration and reform after the Taiping Rebellion.
  • 四书 - Refers to the four Confucian classics: *The Great Learning*, *The Doctrine of the Mean*, *The Analects*, and *Mencius*.
  • 中式 - Refers to passing the examination and being selected for further stages.

Proper Nouns

  • 山阴倪某 - Ni from Shan Yin.
  • 张君 - Zhang.
  • 同治癸酉岁 - Year 1873.
  • 四书 - The four Confucian classics.
  • 中式 - Passing the examination.
  • 冥中事 - Matters in the afterlife.

[407]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 幼时读书家塾 - Literally 'when young, reading at home,' indicating the early years spent studying in a private school.
  • 斗蟋蟀，角胜负 - Fighting crickets, competing for victory, a traditional pastime in China.
  • 巨喙而黑质 - Large beak and black body, describing the physical characteristics of the cricket.
  • 夜闭之笼中，而置诸庭 - At night, closed in a cage, and placed in the courtyard, indicating the care taken for the cricket.
  • 两股断焉 - Its two legs were broken, describing the cricket's condition upon discovery.
  • 懊恨殊甚 - Deeply regretful and angry, expressing the intensity of the emotion.
  • 迹其所在 - Trace its location, indicating the search for the missing part of the cricket.
  • 得一股于石罅 - Found one leg in a crevice of a stone, describing the discovery.
  • 群蚁曳之，将入于穴 - A group of ants dragging it, about to enter the nest, indicating the cause of the cricket's death.
  • 为之抚掌称快 - Clapped hands and rejoiced, expressing satisfaction after the revenge.
  • 遂不复畜蟋蟀 - Thereafter, no longer kept crickets, indicating a change in behavior.
  • 诣将军辕门求见 - Visited the general's gate to request an audience, indicating the action taken by the commoner.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乐平汪宗伯守和 - Wang Zongbo, a high-ranking official from Leping, referring to a historical figure.
  • 嘉庆庚午岁 - In the year Gengwu of the Jiaqing era, a specific year during the Qing dynasty.
  • 少詹事 - Vice President of the Imperial Academy, a high-ranking official position.
  • 奉天府丞 - Governor of the Capital, a high-ranking administrative position.
  • 学政 - Director of Education, responsible for educational affairs.
  • 盛京将军 - General of the Shengjing, a military position in the Qing dynasty.
  • 萨公 - General Sa, referring to a historical figure.
  • 布衣客 - A commoner, referring to a person of low social status.

Proper Nouns

  • 乐平 - Leping, a place name.
  • 汪宗伯守和 - Wang Zongbo, a historical figure.
  • 嘉庆庚午 - Jiaqing Gengwu, a specific year during the Qing dynasty.
  • 少詹事 - Vice President of the Imperial Academy, an official position.
  • 奉天府丞 - Governor of the Capital, an official position.
  • 学政 - Director of Education, an official position.
  • 盛京 - Shengjing, a place name.
  • 萨公 - General Sa, a historical figure.
  • 布衣客 - A commoner, a social status.

[408]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 長揖就坐 - Formal gesture of greeting and taking one's place, combining a bow and sitting down.
  • 古貌俨然 - Describes an appearance that is old-fashioned and dignified.
  • 以生人为冥吏 - Refers to being appointed as a ghost official while still alive.
  • 数至冥中决狱 - Means 'often going to the underworld to judge cases.'
  • 宜了结 - Means 'should be settled' or 'should be concluded.'
  • 固端人 - Means 'is indeed a righteous person.'
  • 先世积累颇厚 - Refers to the ancestors having accumulated significant wealth or merit.
  • 右之 - Means 'to favor' or 'to support.'
  • 与汪善 - Means 'is good friends with Wang.'
  • 如汪有疾 - Means 'if Wang has an illness.'
  • 属其家人勿相惊扰 - Means 'instruct his family not to disturb him.'
  • 亦勿投药饵 - Means 'also do not give him medicine.'
  • 谨守之 - Means 'take care of him carefully.'
  • 三日自愈也 - Means 'he will recover on his own in three days.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 冥吏 - Refers to officials in the underworld, a concept from Chinese folklore and religious beliefs.
  • 冥中 - Refers to the underworld or afterlife realm.
  • 决狱 - Refers to judging or resolving legal cases.
  • 悬四十余年 - Refers to a case that has been unresolved for over forty years.
  • 仇家屡讼不休 - Refers to enemies repeatedly filing lawsuits without resolution.
  • 端人 - Refers to a righteous or upright person.
  • 先世积累颇厚 - Refers to the ancestors having accumulated significant wealth or merit, often associated with Confucian ideals of filial piety and ancestor veneration.
  • 右之 - In this context, it means to favor or support someone, often used in legal or political contexts.

Proper Nouns

  • 萨公 - Refers to a person named Sa, likely a historical figure or a fictional character.
  • 汪学使 - Refers to a person named Wang, who holds the title of Xueshi (a scholar-official).

[409]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 长官命传诸讼者 - Formal expression meaning 'the magistrate ordered the summoning of all litigants.'
  • 焦头烂额者无虑数千人 - Vivid idiom meaning 'those with burnt heads and melted faces, numbering in the thousands,' likely referring to victims of a disaster or conflict.
  • 环呼索命 - Means 'to cry out for vengeance in a circle,' indicating a collective demand for justice or retribution.
  • 婉言谕之 - Means 'to gently advise or persuade them,' emphasizing the magistrate's diplomatic approach.
  • 童子无知 - Means 'the child was unaware or ignorant,' referring to the innocence or lack of understanding of the child involved.
  • 罔识轻重 - Means 'failed to recognize the gravity of the situation,' indicating a lack of judgment.
  • 降其科名 - Means 'to reduce his rank or title,' referring to the punishment of the child.
  • 减其禄算 - Means 'to decrease his lifespan or fortune,' indicating a reduction in his life expectancy or luck.
  • 美中不足 - Means 'imperfections in perfection,' referring to the idea that even in success, there are flaws.
  • 幺麽微物 - Means 'tiny and insignificant creatures,' referring to the insects in a derogatory manner.
  • 贪慕腥膻 - Means 'greedily craving for animal flesh,' describing the insects' desire for food.
  • 污生人之厨灶 - Means 'to defile the kitchens of the living,' referring to the insects' intrusion into human spaces.
  • 侵死者之墓隧 - Means 'to intrude into the tombs of the dead,' indicating the insects' presence in burial grounds.
  • 喧哗 - Means 'to make a loud noise or clamor,' describing the insects' collective protest.
  • 殆将用武 - Means 'nearly ready to resort to violence,' indicating the insects' threat of physical confrontation.
  • 膺冥罚 - Means 'to receive divine punishment,' referring to the child's punishment as a form of divine retribution.
  • 蔽辜 - Means 'to cover or atone for guilt,' suggesting that the punishment is sufficient to absolve the child of blame.
  • 苏 - Means 'to revive or awaken,' referring to the protagonist's return to consciousness.
  • 甫入 - Means 'as soon as entering,' indicating the immediate timing of the encounter.
  • 不交一言 - Means 'without exchanging a word,' describing the lack of communication between the protagonist and the previous guest.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 长官 - Refers to a magistrate or local official, a key figure in traditional Chinese governance.
  • 讼者 - Refers to litigants or those involved in legal disputes, a common theme in Chinese legal and moral narratives.
  • 冥罚 - Refers to divine punishment, a concept rooted in Chinese religious and philosophical traditions.
  • 汪某 - Likely a proper name, possibly referring to the child or another figure in the narrative.
  • 将军 - Refers to a military leader, a figure of authority in Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

  • 长官 - The magistrate or local official.
  • 汪某 - A proper name, likely referring to the child or another figure in the narrative.
  • 将军 - A military leader.

[410]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 薩公迎慰之曰 - Sa Gong greeted and comforted him, saying.
  • 昨日對簿甚勞 - Yesterday, you were very tired from appearing in court. '對簿' refers to appearing in court or facing judgment.
  • 以客言告 - Told him what the guest had said. '以...告' means to inform or tell.
  • 缕述前事 - Described the events in detail. '缕述' means to recount in detail.
  • 冥中决狱者 - The judge in the afterlife. '冥中' means in the afterlife or underworld, and '决狱' means to judge or decide a case.
  • 懔懔 - With awe and fear, describing a feeling of reverence and fear.
  • 祖德所庇 - Protected by the virtue of my ancestors. '祖德' means the virtue of one's ancestors, and '庇' means protection.
  • 生还之望 - Hope of returning to life. '生还' means to return to life.
  • 裒然居首 - Stood out as the first. '裒然' means to stand out or be prominent.
  • 仁庙 - The benevolent temple, a respectful term for the emperor's temple.
  • 一甲第二名 - The second place in the first class, referring to the ranking in the imperial examination.
  • 协揆 - A high-ranking official, often translated as a grand councilor or chancellor.
  • 竟不果 - Ultimately did not succeed. '竟' means ultimately, and '不果' means did not succeed.
  • 道光十六年薨于位 - Died in office in the sixteenth year of Daoguang's reign. '薨' means to die (for nobles), and '于位' means in office.
  • 宣庙 - The temple of the emperor, a respectful term for the emperor's temple.
  • 悼惜 - Mourned and lamented, expressing deep sorrow and regret.
  • 赠太子太保 - Posthumously granted the title of Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince, a high honor.
  • 赐祭葬 - Bestowed a funeral sacrifice and burial, a special honor for high-ranking officials.
  • 故事 - According to custom, referring to traditional practices.
  • 尚书殁于官 - When a minister dies in office. '尚书' means minister, and '殁于官' means dies in office.
  • 得予谥 - Is eligible for a posthumous title. '谥' means posthumous title.
  • 礼臣以请 - The minister of rites requested it. '礼臣' means the minister of rites.
  • 会畿辅旱 - When the capital region suffered from drought. '畿辅' means the capital region, and '旱' means drought.
  • 宵旰忧勤 - Worked tirelessly day and night. '宵旰' means from dawn to dusk, and '忧勤' means to work hard.
  • 此奏久留中 - This request was left pending for a long time. '留中' means to leave pending.
  • 卒不得谥 - Ultimately did not receive a posthumous title. '卒' means ultimately, and '不得谥' means did not receive a posthumous title.
  • 殆即所谓降科名，减禄算，美中不足者欤 - Perhaps this is what is meant by being demoted in rank, reduced in salary, and having some imperfections in perfection. '殆' means perhaps, '降科名' means being demoted in rank, '减禄算' means reduced in salary, and '美中不足' means having some imperfections in perfection.
  • 戕群蚁 - Killed a swarm of ants. '戕' means to kill, and '群蚁' means a swarm of ants.
  • 罹冥谴 - Suffered punishment in the afterlife. '罹' means to suffer, and '冥谴' means punishment in the afterlife.
  • 物命至重 - The value of life is extremely important. '物命' means the value of life, and '至重' means extremely important.
  • 可不慎欤 - How can one be careless? '可不慎' means how can one be careless, and '欤' is an interrogative particle.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 薩公 - Refers to a person named Sa Gong, likely a high-ranking official.
  • 乾隆壬子年 - The year 1772 AD, the third year of the Qianlong Emperor's reign.
  • 嘉庆丙辰岁 - The year 1816 AD, the third year of the Jiaqing Emperor's reign.
  • 仁庙 - Refers to the temple of the emperor, a respectful term for the emperor's temple.
  • 一甲第二名 - Refers to the second place in the first class in the imperial examination.
  • 协揆 - Refers to a high-ranking official, often translated as a grand councilor or chancellor.
  • 道光十六年 - The year 1836 AD, the sixteenth year of the Daoguang Emperor's reign.
  • 宣庙 - Refers to the temple of the emperor, a respectful term for the emperor's temple.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '库藏陈友谅残甲，上缀锈铁六片，各长二寸，广寸，厚不及分' describes the armor in detail, including its dimensions and condition. The syntax is straightforward but requires careful translation to maintain the descriptive nature.
  • The sentence '夥颐窃据，久应火灭烟销' contrasts Chen Youliang's usurpation of power with the eventual destruction of his legacy. The syntax is simple but requires attention to the contrast being made.
  • The sentence '腐朽之余，犹不容触忤，岂以其取精用宏，故厉气亦结而不散乎' explains why the remnants of the armor are still dangerous, suggesting that the essence of the armor is still potent. The syntax is complex and requires careful handling to maintain the logical flow.
  • The sentence '古物流传既久，能为神怪' explains that ancient objects can become supernatural beings due to their long history. The syntax is straightforward but requires attention to the metaphorical meaning.
  • The sentence '深广皆倍于今制，以竹为之，竹皆作┇字形' describes the dimensions and construction of the sedan chairs. The syntax is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the descriptive nature.
  • The sentence '触之者，必头痛身热，故久而不毁' explains why the sedan chairs have not been destroyed, suggesting that touching them causes physical discomfort. The syntax is simple but requires attention to the causal relationship.
  • The sentence '粤贼之乱，有欲薪之者' describes the Taiping Rebellion and the desire to use the sedan chairs as fuel. The syntax is clear but requires attention to the historical context.
  • The sentence '孔子曰物老则为怪，岂不信夫' quotes Confucius to support the idea that old objects can become strange or supernatural. The syntax is straightforward but requires attention to the quotation and its context.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 河南颍州人李麟生玉方 - Classical biographical phrase meaning 'Li Lin of Yingshou, Henan, gave birth to Yu Fang.'
  • 以县令需次江苏 - 'Because he was waiting for an official post as county magistrate in Jiangsu.' *需次* refers to a temporary assignment or waiting period for an official post.
  • 自言 - 'He himself said' or 'he claimed,' indicating a first-person narrative or report.
  • 至桐城一亲串家 - 'Visited the home of a relative in Tongcheng.' *亲串* refers to a close relative or family friend.
  • 其家适请狐仙 - 'His family happened to invite a fox spirit.' *适* here is used to indicate that the event occurred at that time.
  • 巫至，先相度坐处 - 'The witch arrived and first measured out the seating area.' *相度* means to measure or assess the location.
  • 分前后二室 - 'Divided into two rooms, front and back.' This is a clear spatial description.
  • 前室陈果饵，必精美，后室则粗具蔬果而已 - 'The front room was adorned with fruits and delicacies, necessarily fine, while the back room had only coarse vegetables and fruit.' *粗具* means 'coarse provisions' or 'simple food.'
  • 盖以待其从者也 - 'This was to accommodate her attendants.' *盖* is a conjunction meaning 'for the reason that' or 'in order to.'
  • 甲夜仙至 - 'At the first night, the spirit arrived.' *甲夜* refers to the first night of the night, or the early part of the night.
  • 闻其语不见其人 - 'Hearing her voice but not seeing her form.' This is a common expression to describe an unseen but audible presence.
  • 俄而后室中哺啜之声大作 - 'Soon after, loud sounds of eating and drinking came from the back room.' *哺啜* refers to eating and drinking.
  • 前之人责之曰 - 'The person in front scolded her, saying...' This is a narrative construction.
  • 内竟寂然 - 'Inside, it became completely silent.' *竟* emphasizes the suddenness or completeness of the silence.
  • 乃诘主人所问何事，一一酬答，既毕辞去 - 'Then they asked the host what matter had been discussed, and one by one they answered; after that, they departed.' *诘* means to question, *酬答* is to answer in turn.
  • 其来时置梯于中庭 - 'When she came, she placed a ladder in the courtyard.' *置梯* is to place a ladder.
  • 年可十六七，容貌失丽，而身材甚短，较之常人不过三之二而已 - 'They were about sixteen or seventeen years old, with beautiful appearances, but their stature was very short, no more than two-thirds the height of an ordinary person.' *容貌失丽* is a rare character, likely a variant of *美* (beautiful) or *丽* (beautiful), though the character is unclear. *三之二* is 'two-thirds.'
  • 仙畏灯烛光，虽香烟亦避之 - 'The spirit feared the light of lamps and candles, and even avoided incense smoke.' *畏* is to fear, *避之* is to avoid it.
  • 惜麟生方龆龀，不能言其详，但记大略如此 - 'Unfortunately, Lin was still a child at the time, and could not recount the details, but only remembers the general outline as such.' *龆龀* refers to the age of a child, literally 'the age of falling teeth.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 河南颍州 - Henan Province, Yingshou (modern-day Yingshou, Anhui). This is a geographic location in classical Chinese.
  • 桐城 - Tongcheng, a city in modern-day Anhui Province, known historically for its literary tradition.
  • 狐仙 - 'Fox spirit' or 'fox deity,' a common figure in Chinese folklore, often associated with supernatural abilities and sometimes with human-like forms.
  • 女巫 - 'Female witch' or 'female shaman.' In classical Chinese, *巫* refers to a shaman or medium who communicates with spirits.
  • 仙 - 'Spirit' or 'immortal,' here used to refer to the fox spirit.
  • 中庭 - 'Central courtyard,' a common architectural feature in traditional Chinese homes.
  • 拾级而登 - 'Climb the steps,' a classical way of describing ascending a staircase.
  • 龆龀 - A term referring to childhood, literally 'the age when teeth fall out.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有人告曰 - A formal way to introduce a narrative or report, meaning 'someone reported that' or 'it was said that.'
  • 此为少海 - A metaphorical phrase where '少海' (Shao Hai) refers to a small sea or a specific body of water, often used in classical texts to denote a significant or sacred place.
  • 上帝命唐龙门王勃在此修《金台仙录》 - A complex sentence structure that can be broken down as follows: 'The Supreme Deity commanded. Tang Longmen Wang Bo, to compile the *Jintai Xianlu* (Record of the Golden Platform Immortals) here.'
  • 俄而王出 - 'Soon after, Wang emerged.' The word '俄而' (e'er) indicates a short period of time.
  • 梦中以《王集》多散佚 - 'In a dream, he lamented that many of his works were lost or scattered.'
  • 求观其全 - 'He requested to see the complete version.'
  • 王抽架上一帙示之 - 'Wang took a volume from the shelf and showed it to him.'
  • 是《太极宫飞霞阁铭》 - 'It was the *Taixing Gong Feixia Ge Ming* (Inscription of the Flying Cloud Pavilion in the Palace of Supreme Harmony).'
  • 有序文甚长，可数万字 - 'The preface and main text were very long, totaling tens of thousands of characters.'
  • 读已，王命送出 - 'After reading, Wang ordered him to be sent away.'
  • 闻舟中人语而寤 - 'He woke up hearing people talking on the boat.'
  • 则舟进烟台口矣 - 'And the boat had already entered the mouth of Yantai.'
  • 又一事云 - 'Another incident is said.'
  • 戊辰岁在苏州 - 'In the year of Wu Chen, when he was in Suzhou.'
  • 梦乡人箫鼓赛神 - 'He dreamed of people from his hometown playing flutes and drums to worship the gods.'
  • 问何神 - 'He asked what god it was.'
  • 梦中为作祭文 - 'In his dream, he composed an offering text.'
  • 觉而记其一联云 - 'Upon waking, he remembered one couplet, saying:'
  • ‘雄狐定晋，兆霸迹于韩原；雌雉兴秦，膺灵符于陈宝。’ - 'A male fox determined the fate of Jin, foretelling the rise of a hegemon at Han Yuan; a female pheasant brought prosperity to Qin, receiving the divine sign at Chen Bao.'
  • 忆《封禅书》 - 'Recalling the *Fengchan Shu* (Book of Sacrifices to Heaven and Earth).'
  • 祠陈宝是秦文公 - 'The temple of Chen Bao was dedicated to Qin Wen Gong.'
  • 颇悔用事之失 - 'He deeply regretted the mistake in his use of events.'
  • 后读《索隐》引《列异传》 - 'Later, he read *Suo Yin* (Hidden Meaning) which cited *Lieyi Zhuan* (Record of Strange Events).'
  • 则作秦穆公 - 'It mentioned Qin Mu Gong instead.'
  • 梦语固不误也 - 'The dream was indeed accurate.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 南府城外商山西北隅 - A locative phrase indicating a specific location: 'the northwest corner of the Shangshan mountain outside the southern city wall.'
  • 雖碑志無考 - 'Although the stele and inscriptions are unverifiable' or 'though the epitaphs are unrecorded.'
  • 草間石獸猶存 - 'Stone beasts still remain in the grass' — a common feature in ancient tombs, symbolizing the tomb's presence.
  • 故老相传 - 'According to the old stories passed down by the elders' — a common way to introduce folklore or oral tradition.
  • 妃即圆圆 - 'The concubine is none other than Yuan Yuan' — a way of identifying a historical or legendary figure.
  • 吴梅村所为作《圆圆曲》者也 - 'The one for whom Wu Meichun composed the Yuan Yuan Qu (Ballad of Yuan Yuan)' — a reference to the famous poet Wu Meichun and his work.
  • 嘉庆间 - 'During the Jiaqing period' — a reference to the reign of Emperor Jiaqing of the Qing Dynasty.
  • 苏州郑生客游滇 - 'A man from Suzhou named Zheng, a traveler in Yunnan' — introduces a character and his journey.
  • 春日踏青商山 - 'In spring, he went to the Shangshan mountain for a spring outing' — a traditional Chinese custom of visiting scenic places in spring.
  • 访圆圆墓不得 - 'He failed to find Yuan Yuan's tomb' — indicates the tomb's unlocated or hidden nature.
  • 崩榛荒葛中 - 'Among the broken brambles and wild vines' — a poetic description of a desolate and overgrown landscape.
  • 忽迷归路 - 'Suddenly lost his way back' — a sudden turn of events.
  • 俄而落照西沉 - 'Soon the sun set in the west' — a poetic description of the time of day.
  • 暮烟笼树 - 'Evening smoke cloaked the trees' — a common image in classical Chinese poetry.
  • 似有人家 - 'It seemed as though there was a house' — a hint of a hidden or supernatural place.
  • 思往借宿 - 'Thought to go and ask for lodging' — a common action in travel narratives.
  • 朱门洞开 - 'The vermilion gate stood open' — a metaphor for a noble or grand entrance.
  • 玉金铺 - 'Jade thresholds and golden inlays' — a description of luxurious architecture.
  • 俨然王侯第宅 - 'Clearly the residence of a prince or noble' — a description of a grand estate.
  • 乃使阍者转达 - 'He sent the gatekeeper to relay the message' — a formal process of introduction.
  • 导入东厢 - 'Led into the eastern wing' — a reference to the layout of a traditional Chinese mansion.
  • 为设食樽酒，簋贰亦极精洁 - 'He was served food, wine, and vessels, all of which were extremely refined and clean' — a description of a lavish banquet.
  • 饭已 - 'After the meal' — a common transition in narrative.
  • 老妪出问 - 'An old woman came out to ask' — a traditional way of introducing a character.
  • 客操吴音 - 'The guest speaks the Wu dialect' — a way of identifying the speaker's origin.
  • 是何乡贯 - 'What is your hometown?' — a standard question in classical Chinese.
  • 少选，妪秉烛而出 - 'A short while later, the old woman came out with a candle' — a poetic description of the evening.
  • 肃客登堂 - 'She respectfully led the guest into the hall' — a formal gesture of hospitality.
  • 羽服霓衣 - 'Feathered robe and rainbow clothing' — a description of a mystical or celestial appearance.
  • 如女冠装束 - 'Like a nun's attire' — indicating a religious or monastic figure.
  • 降阶而迎 - 'Descended the steps to greet' — a formal and respectful gesture.
  • 妾即邢氏 - 'I am none other than the Xing family' — a self-introduction by a woman.
  • 埋香地下，百有余年 - 'Buried in the earth, for over a hundred years' — a poetic way of expressing time passed.
  • 时移物换，丘陇就平 - 'Times have changed, things have transformed; the hills and mounds have become level' — a lament for the passage of time and the decay of the tomb.
  • 念君是妾同乡 - 'I remember that you are from my hometown' — a personal connection.
  • 有小诗十首，求为传播 - 'I have ten small poems, which I ask you to spread' — a request for the guest to disseminate the poems.
  • 因命侍女取诗付郑 - 'So she ordered the attendant to fetch the poems and give them to Zheng' — the conclusion of the encounter.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 邢妃坟 - 'The tomb of Consort Xing' — refers to a historical or legendary concubine, likely from the Ming Dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 土人言往往見山市 - The locals say that they often see *shanshi* (mountain market). *Shanshi* is a classical Chinese term for a *fata morgana* or mirage, often described as a ghostly city or market appearing in the distance.
  • 聞是山多金苗 - He heard that this mountain has many *jinmiao* (gold veins). *Jinmiao* is a term used in mining or prospecting to refer to potential gold deposits or mineral-rich areas.

  • 脫粟一餐，不責直也 - 'A meal of coarse rice, without asking for payment.' *Tuo su* is a humble meal, and *bu ze zhi* means 'not demanding payment.'
  • 客不如佣于我家 - 'You would be better off working for my family.' This is a pragmatic suggestion offered by the old man.

  • 怅然思归 - 'Feeling melancholy and longing to return home.'
  • 暂还当复来耳 - 'You may return temporarily, but you should come back again.' This is a polite and encouraging statement from the old man.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 金苗 - A term used in Chinese mining and prospecting to describe gold-bearing areas or mineral-rich veins.
  • 山市 - *Shanshi* is a classical Chinese term for a *fata morgana* or mirage, often described as a ghostly city or market appearing in the distance. It appears in classical Chinese literature and is often associated with mystical or supernatural phenomena.
  • 脫粟 - 'Coarse rice' or 'simple meal,' often used in classical Chinese to describe a humble or unadorned meal.
  • 佣于我家 - 'Work for my family.' This reflects the social structure of traditional Chinese society, where service or labor for a household was a common form of employment.
  • 腊日 - *Laxi* (the 23rd day of the 12th lunar month), a traditional Chinese festival for honoring ancestors and preparing for the Lunar New Year. It was also a time for cleaning, preparing food, and making offerings.

  • 怅然思归 - A common emotional expression in classical Chinese literature, conveying a sense of longing and melancholy for one’s home or family.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence is elliptical and can be read as 'If the guest insists on returning, please let it be tomorrow.'
  • This clause is complex and can be read as 'A modest provision of wages, not abundant, but sufficient for your family to enjoy several years of warmth and fullness.'
  • This clause is conditional and can be read as 'However, it must be kept secret, not even to be revealed to the person in the bed.'
  • This sentence is a sequence of actions and can be read as 'Just as midnight approached, the old man urged the bear to rise and said, 'It is suitable to travel under the moonlight.''
  • This sentence is a sequence of actions and can be read as 'He placed several steamed buns in the bear's chest and said, 'If you are hungry, eat these; you can reach home.''
  • This clause is a conditional and can be read as 'Still come here after the Lantern Festival, do not break the appointment!'
  • This sentence is a sequence of actions and observations and can be read as 'The bear just stepped out, and the door immediately closed. Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sequence '筒中储各兽之毛，犬、豕、牛、马、驴、骡皆备' must be read as a single catalogue of the types of animal hair stored in the tube.
  • The clause '暗中拈得一毛，其毛为何物，魂即化是物' describes the process of picking a hair and the resulting transformation.
  • The clause '出至旷野，迷罔行人，往往受其累' describes the actions of the transformed entity and the consequences of encountering it.
  • The clause '间或遇有胆力者，执而捶击之，则哀鸣乞怜' describes how the entity is dealt with and its reaction.
  • The clause '天欲明，窘更甚，辄自言其姓氏里居，人或杀之，则病者死矣' describes the urgency of the situation and the fatal outcome of killing the entity.
  • The clause '病人所流出之黄水，人稍沾染之，即中其毒，病亦如之，而病者得以稍间' describes the nature of the disease and its symptoms.
  • The clause '故凡有是病者，相戒不食于其家，或不得已而就食，则以赤铜置食器内' describes the precautionary measures taken to avoid the disease.
  • The clause '异哉！此疾视粤之麻疯、粤之畜蛊更有甚矣' compares the severity of the disease to other known conditions.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 咸丰间 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Xianfeng (1850–1861), a period marked by the Taiping Rebellion and significant political and social upheaval.
  • 功令 - A term for official regulations or decrees, often issued by the imperial court, particularly during the Qing dynasty.
  • 服满 - Refers to the completion of the traditional mourning period, which was 25 months for a parent. However, in this case, the individual is still required to observe a shorter, formal mourning period (百日服).
  • 百日服 - A 100-day mourning period, often observed for non-parental relatives or as a formal gesture of respect.
  • 知府 - A prefect or governor of a prefecture, a mid-level administrative official in the Qing bureaucracy.
  • 满洲人 - Refers to Manchus, the ruling ethnic group of the Qing dynasty.
  • 妾 - A concubine, a secondary wife in a polygamous household.
  • 投井死 - A traditional method of suicide in ancient China, often associated with female virtue or shame.
  • 异姓昆弟之谊 - A term used to describe a close bond between individuals of different surnames, akin to sworn brothers or close friends.
  • 王媪 - Likely refers to the wife of Mr. Wang, or the mother of Lian.
  • 遣戍 - A form of punishment in which an individual was exiled to a remote military post, common in the Qing dynasty.
  • 羁纟曳 - A term that may be a variant or miswritten form of “羁旅” (traveling with luggage) or “纟曳” (a bundle or pack).
  • 主母 - Refers to the wife of the household’s master, often the senior female figure in a family.
  • 以女畜之 - To raise someone as a daughter, indicating that Lian was formally adopted or treated as a daughter.
  • 委禽 - A traditional term for a marriage proposal, literally 'to present a fowl' as a symbolic gesture of marriage.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '廣陵婦謬相敬重，不敢均禮' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'The woman from Guangling was mistakenly shown great respect, but she dared not treat her equally.'
  • The clause '微用其離間之策，而德甚愛蓮，不能動' is complex, suggesting that despite the woman's manipulative tactics, 'De' remains deeply attached to Lotus.
  • The phrase '廣陵婦乃益自卑下，日為' is incomplete but implies that the woman from Guangling further humbles herself daily.
  • The clause '惟德則聞之，不知其何術也' is ambiguous, as it can be read as 'Only De can smell it, but she doesn't know how she does it' or 'Only De can smell it, but she doesn't know what her method is.'
  • The sentence '德遂不復入蓮室，久而厭之，乃以与其弟' is complex, indicating that 'De' eventually stops entering Lotus's room, grows tired of her, and decides to give her to her brother.
  • The clause '弟于兄弟行居八，家人呼之曰八爷' is elliptical but indicates the brother's position in the family and the family's affectionate address for him.
  • The phrase '蓮号泣不从，德语之曰' is incomplete but suggests that Lotus cries and refuses to comply, and 'De' speaks to her.
  • The phrase '吾与若缘尽矣' is elliptical but suggests that the relationship between 'De' and Lotus has ended.
  • The phrase '从八爷不胜从我乎' is elliptical but suggests that following the brother is better than following 'De.'
  • The phrase '择吉日，将使成礼' is elliptical but indicates the selection of a good day for a formal ceremony.
  • The clause '而所谓八爷者，亦喜甚，未及期先入莲室' is complex, suggesting that the brother, who is called 'Eighth Master,' is very happy and enters Lotus's room before the scheduled time.
  • The phrase '以甘言悦之曰' is elliptical but indicates that the brother uses sweet words to please Lotus.
  • The phrase '若意中欲何好物？当为若购之' is elliptical but suggests that the brother asks Lotus what good thing she wants and promises to buy it for her.
  • The phrase '莲不应，固问之' is incomplete but suggests that Lotus does not respond, and the brother persistently asks her.
  • The phrase '乃大言曰' is incomplete but suggests that Lotus then speaks loudly.
  • The phrase '他无所需，为我买好棺一具足矣' is elliptical but suggests that Lotus says she has no other needs, but she wants a good coffin.
  • The phrase '八爷悚然而出' is incomplete but suggests that the brother leaves in alarm.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 笃信神仙修炼之术 - Classical expression referring to Daoist spiritual practices aimed at achieving immortality.
  • 事吕仙甚虔 - Worshipped the immortal Lü very devoutly. *事* means to serve or worship, and *甚虔* means 'very devoutly.'
  • 不得一见为恨 - Regretted not being able to see him once. Common classical expression of deep longing.
  • 冀有所遇 - Hoped to meet someone. *冀* is a classical expression meaning 'hope' or 'aspire.'
  • 素识之某乙 - A person named Yi whom he had known before. *素识* is a classical term meaning 'previously known.'
  • 梨园中脚色也 - A performer in the Pear Garden. *梨园* refers to the imperial music and drama school, and *脚色* is a classical term for actors or performers.
  • 扮一吕仙可乎 - Can I dress up as Lü the Immortal for you? *扮* is a classical verb meaning 'to dress up' or 'to impersonate.'
  • 星冠霓衣 - Starry crown and rainbow robe. Classical description of the attire of immortals or deities.
  • 背负长剑，手执尘尾 - Carrying a long sword on his back and holding a dust whisk in his hand. *尘尾* is a classical object, a type of whisk used by Daoist immortals or scholars.
  • 君视吾似吕仙否 - Do you think I look like Lü the Immortal? A rhetorical question used to confirm the illusion.
  • 然则吕仙亦不过如此 - Then Lü the Immortal is no more than this. Philosophical or ironic statement about the nature of immortality.
  • 求见奚为 - Why then seek to see him? *奚为* is a classical expression meaning 'why do something?' or 'what is the use of it?'
  • 此去十许里 - About ten miles from here. *十许里* is a classical way of expressing approximate distance.
  • 方演剧 - A play is being performed. *演剧* is a classical term for staging a play.
  • 待吾登场，吾去矣 - Wait until I take the stage, then I will go. *登场* is a classical term used in the context of theater or performance.
  • 解所服佩，仍置囊中，负之而去 - He removed the clothes and ornaments, put them back in the bag, and left. *解所服佩* is a classical expression meaning 'removed the attire and ornaments.'
  • 惘然久之 - He remained in a daze for a long time. *惘然* is a classical term for confusion or bewilderment.
  • 将睹优 - Intending to see the actors. *优* is a classical term for actors or performers.
  • 寂无所见 - Nothing was to be seen. *寂* is a classical term for silence or emptiness.
  • 甚怪之 - Found it very strange. *甚* is an intensifier, *怪之* is a classical expression for 'found it strange.'
  • 何诳我 - Why did you deceive me? *诳* is a classical term for deception or lying.
  • 初未遇君 - I had not met you at that time. *初未* is a classical expression meaning 'had not yet.'
  • 失之交臂 - Missed the opportunity by a hair’s breadth. Classical idiom meaning to come close to something but miss it.

  • 西岭汤王庙 - A temple dedicated to the god of hot springs, likely referring to the legendary King Tang of Shang, who is associated with the discovery of hot springs. *西岭* is a geographical feature, possibly a mountain or hill.
  • 梨园中脚色 - *梨园* (Pear Garden) refers to the imperial music and drama school established during the Tang Dynasty, and *脚色* refers to actors or performers. This alludes to the traditional Chinese theater system.
  • 尘尾 - A type of whisk made from the tail of a fox or other animal, commonly used by Daoist immortals and scholars as a symbol of wisdom or elegance.
  • 失之交臂 - Classical idiom meaning 'to miss an opportunity by a hair’s breadth,' indicating a near miss or a moment of almost achieving something.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 舅負其甥錢二十萬者 - The uncle owes his nephew 200,000 units of currency; '負' means 'to owe' or 'to be in debt.'
  • 死則為牛以償耳 - A superstitious belief that after death, the uncle will reincarnate as a cow to repay the debt; '死則' means 'after death,' and '為牛以償耳' means 'will become a cow to repay [the debt].'
  • 不以常牛畜之 - The nephew does not treat the cow as an ordinary animal; '不以常牛' means 'not as an ordinary cow,' and '畜之' means 'treat it as livestock.'
  • 每出游，必與俱 - The nephew always takes the cow with him when he goes out; '每出游' means 'every time he goes out,' and '必與俱' means 'always brings it along.'
  • 碎其盆盎 - The cow accidentally breaks the old man's pots and jars; '碎' means 'break,' and '盆盎' refers to pottery items like pots and jars.
  • 叟異其言 - The old man is surprised by what the nephew says; '異其言' means 'finds his words strange or unusual.'
  • 欣然而去 - The old man leaves happily; '欣然' means 'happily' or 'joyfully,' and '去' means 'to leave.'
  • 長鳴而毙 - The cow lets out a long cry before dying; '長鳴' means 'a long cry,' and '而毙' means 'and then dies.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • The story involves a debt between family members, reflecting the importance of familial obligations and debts in traditional Chinese society.
  • The belief in reincarnation as a means of repaying debts is rooted in folk beliefs and superstitions common in traditional Chinese culture.
  • The nephew's treatment of the cow reflects a belief in the supernatural connection between the uncle and the cow, which is a common theme in folk tales and legends.
  • The breaking of pottery is a common event in daily life, and the reaction of the old man (being angry and hitting the cow) reflects the value placed on property and the consequences of accidental damage.
  • The old man's departure is marked by a sense of satisfaction, indicating that the debt has been resolved.
  • The cow's death is described in a way that suggests it is a fulfillment of its purpose, reflecting a belief in the interconnectedness of life and death.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 舅負其甥錢二十萬者 - The phrase '負其甥' could be interpreted as 'owing to his nephew' or 'owing his nephew.'
  • 死則為牛以償耳 - The phrase '死則為牛' could be interpreted as 'after death, he will become a cow' or 'after death, he will transform into a cow.'
  • 不以常牛畜之 - The phrase '不以常牛' could be interpreted as 'not treating it as an ordinary cow' or 'not considering it an ordinary cow.'
  • 碎其盆盎 - The phrase '碎其盆盎' could be interpreted as 'breaking the old man's pots and jars' or 'damaging the old man's pots and jars.'
  • 欣然而去 - The phrase '欣然而去' could be interpreted as 'leaving happily' or 'departing joyfully.'
  • 長鳴而毙 - The phrase '長鳴而毙' could be interpreted as 'letting out a long cry and then dying' or 'crying out loudly and then dying.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 五色紙人 - Refers to paper figures of different colors, often used in folk rituals or performances.
  • 畫符呵氣 - '畫符' means drawing a talisman, and '呵氣' means blowing one's breath onto it, both common in traditional Chinese rituals.
  • 五更 - Refers to the fifth watch of the night, a traditional Chinese timekeeping system.
  • 後樓 - Refers to a building in the rear, often used in traditional Chinese architecture.
  • 喧阗 - Describes a noisy or bustling atmosphere, often used in classical Chinese literature to depict lively scenes.
  • 紅光圍繞 - Describes a scene surrounded by red light, often associated with supernatural or divine presence in Chinese folklore.
  • 真火 - Refers to genuine or powerful fire, often used metaphorically to describe intense energy or force.
  • 賢主人 - Refers to a noble or respected master, often used in classical Chinese to denote someone of high status or virtue.
  • 烏有 - Means 'nothing' or 'non-existent,' often used to describe something that has vanished or disappeared.
  • 留別書 - Refers to a farewell letter, often written by someone leaving or departing.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This long sequence describes a series of actions performed by Chen at midnight, including lighting candles, taking out paper figures, arranging them, drawing talismans, and blowing breath onto them.
  • This sequence describes the sudden movement of the paper figures, their slow walking, and the increasing speed, accompanied by a muffled sound like thunder.
  • This sequence describes Chen's statement that the demon has left, followed by the paper figures falling and being hidden.
  • This sequence describes the maid hearing noise from the back building, peeking through a window, and seeing red light surrounding the building.
  • This sequence describes the old man and his wife's conversation about their intention to leave after the maid recovers, but being forced to leave due to Chen's powerful fire, and their regret at not being able to say goodbye to the noble master.
  • This sequence describes the cessation of sound by the fifth watch, the maid entering to see the table and bed unchanged, the floor clean, and everything else gone.
  • This sequence describes the maid opening the small chest and finding a farewell letter from Mr. Zhong, which she reads sadly.

  • 而使以谱香代之 - And made it so that 谱香 replaced the old tutor. This implies a supernatural intervention or command.
  • 已而谱香果以是日至 - Soon after, 谱香 indeed came on that day. '已而' indicates a short passage of time.

  • 于他舍小坐 - He sat in another room. '小坐' means 'to sit for a short time.'
  • 忽一淡巴菰筒破窗而入 - Suddenly, a pipe of tobacco flew through the window. '忽' indicates suddenness.
  • 其声甚厉 - The sound was very loud. '厉' here means 'sharp' or 'loud.'
  • 此筒乃谱香遗在书舍中者 - This pipe was one that 谱香 had left in the study. '遗' means 'left behind.'
  • 取视，则上系一纸书 - Upon picking it up and looking, he saw a note attached to it. '则' here introduces a consequence.
  • 乃亦书数语答之 - He then wrote a few words in response. '乃' here indicates a subsequent action.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 捷速如此 - 捷速 means 'swift' or 'quick,' and 如此 means 'like this' or 'in this manner.' It emphasizes the speed of the action.
  • 宜其一夕之中书满四壁也 - 宜其 means 'it is appropriate that' or 'it is fitting that,' 一夕之中 means 'in one night,' 书满四壁 means 'the walls were covered with writing.' This phrase suggests that it is fitting that the walls were covered with writing in one night.
  • 语意仍不训 - 语意 means 'the meaning of the words,' 仍 means 'still,' and 不训 means 'not understandable' or 'not logical.' It indicates that the meaning of the words was still not clear or logical.

  • 谱香言此狐亦有不可及者 - 谱香 is a name or title, 言 means 'said,' 此狐 means 'this fox,' 亦 means 'also,' 不可及者 means 'unattainable' or 'beyond reach.' It means that Pu Xiang said that this fox also had something unattainable.
  • 部郎一幼子初学为时文 - 部郎 is a title, 一幼子 means 'a young son,' 初学 means 'just learning,' and 为时文 means 'to write current-style essays.' It means that the official's young son was just learning to write current-style essays.

  • 居然成文 - 居然 means 'unexpectedly' or 'suddenly,' and 成文 means 'became a coherent piece of writing.' It means that unexpectedly, it became a coherent piece of writing.

  • 又岁杪 - 又 means 'again,' and 岁杪 means 'the end of the year.' It means that again, at the end of the year.
  • 部郎有故人告贷数十金 - 部郎 is a title, 有故人 means 'had an old friend,' 告贷 means 'asked for a loan,' and 数十金 means 'several dozen gold pieces.' It means that the official had an old friend who asked for a loan of several dozen gold pieces.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 甫搁笔，而覆书已裹小石投入，捷速如此，宜其一夕之中书满四壁也 - This sentence is a complex sequence of actions and consequences. '甫搁笔' introduces the action of putting down the brush, followed by the swift response of the reply being thrown in. The phrase '宜其一夕之中书满四壁也' concludes the sequence, indicating that it is fitting that the walls were covered with writing in one night.
  • 语意仍不训，知不可以理谕，遂不复答 - This sequence of clauses shows a logical progression: the meaning of the words was still not clear, so it was known that reason could not be used to explain, and therefore, no longer did he reply.
  • 未久，辞魏而他就焉 - This clause is a simple narrative progression: not long after, he left Wei and went to him.
  • 谱香言此狐亦有不可及者，部郎一幼子初学为时文，甫作小讲，其文甚劣，几不可读 - This sequence of clauses introduces a new subject (Pu Xiang's comment about the fox) and then shifts to a different narrative (the official's young son's poor writing).
  • 狐为易七八字，居然成文，真谚所谓点铁成金者也 - This sequence of clauses describes the fox's intervention and the surprising result, using a well-known saying to emphasize the transformation.
  • 又岁杪 - This clause indicates a temporal transition, marking the end of the year.

  • 春梦香甜，不知东方之既白 - 春梦香甜 means 'a sweet and pleasant dream,' and 不知东方之既白 means 'not knowing that the east has already turned white,' indicating a long, dreamlike night.
  • 忽毕爆有声，火光焱焱，照耀窗棂 - 忽毕爆有声 describes a sudden loud noise, and 火光焱焱 means 'flames blazing brightly.' 照耀窗棂 means 'illuminating the window frames,' suggesting a sudden, intense light.

  • 审视之，乃枯骸一具也 - 审视之 means 'examining closely,' and 乃枯骸一具也 means 'it turned out to be a withered corpse,' indicating the protagonist's shock.
  • 惊起四顾，则屋宇帷幕了无所睹，独立墟墓间 - 惊起四顾 means 'jumping up and looking around in shock,' and 则屋宇帷幕了无所睹 means 'but the house and its furnishings were nowhere to be seen.' 独立墟墓间 means 'standing alone in a desolate cemetery,' emphasizing the protagonist's isolation.
  • 墓旁故有烧瓦之窑，窑破，故火光进射也 - 墓旁故有烧瓦之窑 means 'there was a kiln for firing tiles beside the grave,' and 窑破，故火光进射也 means 'the kiln had broken, so the flames were shooting out,' explaining the source of the fire.

  • 俄而大病，因与家人言所遇，咸以为虑 - 俄而大病 means 'soon after, he fell seriously ill,' and 因与家人言所遇 means 'he told his family about what he had experienced.' 咸以为虑 means 'they all were concerned,' showing the family's reaction.
  • 卧病月余竟愈 - 卧病月余 means 'lying ill for over a month,' and 竟愈 means 'and eventually recovered,' indicating the protagonist's recovery.
  • 于是尽涤邪念，下帷苦读，卒成进士，入词林焉 - 尽涤邪念 means 'completely rid himself of evil thoughts,' and 下帷苦读 means 'studying diligently behind closed doors.' 卒成进士，入词林焉 means 'eventually became a Jinshi and entered the Hanlin Academy,' indicating the protagonist's transformation and success.

  • 廖归，疑不成寐，已而倦极，恍惚梦至一处，庙宇宏敞 - Describes Liao San's return and his subsequent dream of arriving at a grand temple.

  • 留余饭于署中 - 'Left me a meal at the office'; '署中' refers to the official residence or office of the magistrate.

  • 须乙夜潮至，始行 - 'Must wait until the tide comes at the second watch of the night, before we can depart'; '乙夜' is an ancient timekeeping term referring to the second watch of the night (approximately 2–4 AM).
  • 乃于晚饭后登舟 - 'So I boarded the boat after dinner'; '乃' indicates a sequence of events.
  • 余辞焉不可 - 'I tried to decline, but he would not allow it'; '辞焉不可' is a set phrase indicating a polite refusal that was not accepted.
  • 余时止挈二仆 - 'At that time, I had only two attendants'; '挈' means 'to bring along' or 'to take with.'
  • 其一从余在郡署，其一留守舟中 - A clear division of roles: one was following in the prefectural office, and the other was left to guard the boat.
  • 止能护持行李，竟不能与之争 - 'He could only protect my luggage, but could not compete with the others'; '止能' is a limiting phrase meaning 'only could.'
  • 怒曰：“此何人也？” - 'He angrily said, 'Who are these people?' This is a direct question with emotional weight.
  • 渐东当大乱之后 - 'After a period of great disorder in Jiantong'; '渐东' refers to the area around Taizhou (modern Zhejiang), and '大乱' is a classical term for 'great disorder' or 'great chaos.'
  • 盗贼多有 - 'There were many bandits and robbers'; '盗贼' is a classical term for criminals or bandits.
  • 太守所至，辄有亲兵从行 - 'Whenever the magistrate went somewhere, he always had his personal soldiers accompanying him'; '辄' is a classical adverb meaning 'always' or 'every time.'
  • 遂命亲兵悉驱其人登岸 - 'He then ordered his soldiers to drive all of them ashore'; '遂' indicates a sequence of actions, '悉驱' is a command to 'drive out completely.'
  • 并以四亲兵送余至黄岩焉 - 'And with four of his soldiers, he escorted me to Huangyan'; '并以' is a classical way of saying 'and with,' and '焉' is a sentence-ending particle.
  • 盖台温地僻，舟楫稀少 - 'For Taizhou and Wenzhou are remote places, and boats are scarce'; '盖' is a classical particle meaning 'for' or 'because,' used to introduce a reason.
  • 寻常行客每雇一舟，必有趁船者 - 'Ordinary travelers who hired a boat always had people who would 'ride' the boat'; '趁船者' is a classical term for people who travel without paying, or 'free riders.'
  • 固非尽盗贼也 - 'They were not all bandits, of course'; '固' is a classical particle meaning 'certainly' or 'indeed.'
  • 然其情状，亦甚难堪矣 - 'But their behavior was quite difficult to endure'; '情状' is a classical term for 'circumstances' or 'situation.'
  • 非太守亲送，岂能麾而出之哉！ - 'Without the magistrate personally escorting me, how could they have been driven away?'; '岂能' is a classical way of expressing doubt or rhetorical questioning.
  • 附书于此，俾于役者知所警焉 - 'I am writing this account here so that those who serve may know to be cautious'; '俾' is a classical verb meaning 'to make' or 'to cause,' and '知所警焉' is a set phrase meaning 'know what to be cautious of.'

  • 不聞於官 - Did not report the matter to the authorities; *聞於官* is a classical expression for reporting to the government.

  • 忽見婦來，流血滿身 - Suddenly saw his wife appear, covered in blood; a supernatural appearance.

  • 言已不見 - After speaking, she disappeared; a common phrase in classical Chinese for supernatural appearances.

  • 血殷床褥 - Blood stained the bed and bedding; *殷* is a classical term for deeply staining or soaking.
  • 昼夜呼號，閱兩月而毙 - He cried out day and night, and after two months, he died; *閱* means 'after' or 'during,' and *毙* means to die.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贷富家钱 - Refers to the practice of borrowing money from wealthy families, common in traditional Chinese society.
  • 已偿其本，惟负其息钱一千 - Reflects the financial practices of the time, where loans included both principal and interest.
  • 贫不能偿 - Highlights the economic struggles of the poor in traditional Chinese society.
  • 求免焉 - Shows the desperation of the poor to avoid debt, a common theme in classical literature.
  • 富子执不可 - Illustrates the harshness and lack of compassion often displayed by the wealthy towards the poor.
  • 竟如数取之 - Reflects the strict enforcement of debt repayment, even when the borrower is in dire straits.
  • 卢憾甚 - Conveys the deep resentment and frustration of the poor when faced with unfair treatment.
  • 因思其场圃外积草成阜 - Describes a common agricultural practice of piling up hay or straw for storage.
  • 高数丈 - Emphasizes the height and size of the pile, suggesting a significant amount of hay or straw.
  • 若货之，值钱可十万 - Reflects the economic value of agricultural products and the potential for profit through trade.
  • 乃于幕夜跻其巅 - Uses the image of climbing to the top of a mound at night to create a dramatic and symbolic scene.
  • 掀其盖，置火于中 - Describes a method of burning the pile, which was a common way to dispose of hay or straw.
  • 数日而草不然 - Highlights the unexpected outcome of the fire not spreading, which sets up the subsequent events.
  • 谋诸妇 - Shows the involvement of the wife in decision-making, reflecting the role of women in traditional Chinese households.
  • 适有微雨 - Describes a light rain, which is a common weather phenomenon in many regions of China.
  • 故不也 - Explains why the fire did not spread, adding a layer of realism to the narrative.
  • 宜再置之 - Suggests a logical next step in the plan, showing the persistence of the characters.
  • 乃复跻其巅 - Repeats the action of climbing to the top, reinforcing the dramatic tension.
  • 掀而视焉 - Describes the act of opening and looking, which is a key moment in the story.
  • 则其中为火所烧 - Reveals the unexpected outcome of the fire spreading inside the mound, creating a sense of surprise.
  • 已空矣 - Emphasizes the complete destruction of the pile, highlighting the effectiveness of the fire.
  • 因其盖沾雨 - Explains why the fire did not spread further, adding a layer of explanation to the narrative.
  • 故火不能透 - Concludes the explanation of why the fire did not spread, providing a logical resolution to the situation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '翁怃然曰：“我其死矣！”' is straightforward but the emotional tone of '怃然' needs to be conveyed.
  • The clause '自念衣食粗足，婚嫁俱毕，死亦何憾' is a series of thoughts linked by '自念,' indicating the old man's reflections.
  • The long clause '惟曾与某氏子为媒，此子孑然一身，贫无婚费，女氏恒有悔婚之意' describes a complex situation involving the old man's past actions and their consequences.
  • The clause '我在故不敢言，我死奈何' shows the old man's concern about what will happen after his death.
  • The sentence '悉召诸子而语之，曰：“某氏之子，其父在日，曾假我钱八十万，以相信故无券也。”' is a direct speech block that needs to be clearly marked in English.
  • The clause '今我老矣，久假不归，异日何面见故人于地下乎' is a rhetorical question expressing the old man's concern about his afterlife.
  • The sequence of actions '辇钱而归之，乃为故人子择日娶妇，告期于女氏' needs to be clearly linked in English.
  • The clause '女氏无以拒，遂成婚' shows the resolution of the situation.
  • The sentence '翁喜曰：“我事毕矣。”' is a direct speech block that needs to be clearly marked in English.
  • The sequence of events '越月竟无恙，而其奴又来见，贺曰：“主人不死矣，冥中续有牒至，除去主人之名也。”' needs to be clearly linked in English, with the final clause being a direct speech block.

  • 友云：“我即将之官，不及与故人叙契阔，奈何？” - “之官” means to take up an official post; “叙契阔” is a classical expression from *The Book of Songs* meaning to reminisce or catch up with old friends; “奈何” is an expression of regret or lament.

  • 言已别去 - “言已” means after speaking; “别去” means to part ways or leave.
  • 某君遵旧路回家，则死已逾日，至是复苏 - “遵旧路” means to follow the old path; “逾日” means more than a day; “复苏” means to revive or regain consciousness.
  • 以其事告人，自云不久人世矣，人犹未之信 - “告人” means to tell others; “自云” is he said himself; “不久人世” means not long for this world; “犹未之信” means still did not believe it.

  • 扬州府 - A historical administrative region in Jiangsu Province, China.
  • 叙契阔 - A classical reference from *The Book of Songs* (e.g., *Chu Ci* or *Shijing*), meaning to catch up with old friends after a long time.
  • 引疾归 - A classical expression used by officials to resign from office, often citing illness, but sometimes as a euphemism for being dismissed or disgraced.
  • 槛车 - A type of carriage used to transport prisoners or criminals, often with bars or restraints.
  • 无疾而卒 - A euphemistic way of saying someone died peacefully or naturally, without illness or suffering.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 同年生 - Term used to denote a classmate or fellow examinee in the imperial examination system; literally, 'same year's birth' or 'same year's examinee.'
  • 見其青葱可愛 - '青葱' refers to green onions or leeks, but in this context, it is used metaphorically to mean 'green and fresh' or 'young and attractive.'
  • 若吾他年讀書有成 - Conditional phrase meaning 'if I achieve success in my studies in the future.'
  • 此枝當活 - 'This branch will live' or 'this branch will grow,' used metaphorically to signify success or fulfillment of a wish.
  • 閱三十余年 - 'After more than thirty years,' a time expression.
  • 竟成大树 - 'Ultimately became a large tree,' indicating growth over time.
  • 佥曰 - Collective term meaning 'all said' or 'everyone said.'
  • 此王氏之祥也 - 'This is an omen for the Wang family.'
  • 或言 - 'Some say' or 'there are those who say.'
  • 椒不留子 - 'Pepper does not retain its seeds' or 'pepper does not keep its offspring,' implying that the plant does not produce viable offspring.
  • 其苞一启子即堕落 - 'Once the pod opens, the seeds fall immediately,' describing the plant's reproductive process.
  • 恐不利于子姓 - 'It is feared that this is not beneficial for one's descendants.' '子姓' refers to one's descendants or lineage.
  • 乃议伐之 - 'So they proposed to cut it down.'
  • 其效可睹矣 - 'Its effect is evident.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 寶應城 - Baoying is a historical county in Jiangsu Province, and the text refers to a house located in its eastern gate.
  • 同年生 - A term from the imperial examination system, used to denote those who passed the same examination in the same year.
  • 王文勤公 - A title for a high-ranking official. '文勤' is a posthumous honorific title (谥号) meaning 'diligent in literature and service.' The full name would be Wang Wenzhen (王文勤).
  • 道光丙午 - The year 1836, during the reign of Emperor Daoguang of the Qing Dynasty.
  • 庚戌 - The year 1840, also during the Daoguang era.
  • 同治中 - During the reign of Emperor Tongzhi (1861–1875), a period of reform and modernization in the late Qing.
  • 編修 - A position in the Hanlin Academy, responsible for compiling texts and assisting in official matters.
  • 浙臬 - The governor of Zhejiang’s judicial affairs; '臬' refers to the judicial official of a province.
  • 粤藩 - The governor of Guangdong (Guangdong province).
  • 闽抚 - The governor of Fujian province.
  • 子姓 - A term for one’s descendants or lineage, often used in classical Chinese.
  • 台湾 - Taiwan, then a province of the Qing Dynasty.
  • 福州 - Fuzhou, the capital of Fujian province.
  • 刘夫人 - Lady Liu, the wife of Wang Wenzhen.

Proper Nouns

  • 粤藩 - The governor of Guangdong province.

  • 福州 - Fuzhou, the capital of Fujian province.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 律曰 - Formal statement introducing a legal or customary rule.
  • 妻在，以妾为妻，杖九十 - Specific legal punishment for a man who designates his concubine as his wife while his legal wife is still alive.
  • 若妻已不在，则律无明文，似所不禁 - Conditional statement suggesting that if the legal wife is deceased, the law does not explicitly prohibit designating a concubine as the wife.
  • 命服 - Official robes, often associated with high-ranking officials or their wives.
  • 一品之服 - Robes of the first rank, indicating the highest level of official status.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 妾 - Refers to a concubine, a secondary wife in a traditional Chinese household.
  • 鬼神 - Refers to spirits or deities, often invoked in traditional Chinese culture to explain unexplained phenomena.

Proper Nouns

  • 姚平泉 - The name of the author's maternal uncle, Mr. Yao Pingquan.
  • 陶观察 - The name of a certain Mr. Tao, an official.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 此婢乃六合人 - Literal statement meaning 'this maid is from Liuhe,' introducing the subject.
  • 咸丰中六合失守 - Historical event referring to the fall of Liuhe during the Xianfeng era of the Qing dynasty.
  • 内子姚夫人买得之 - Means 'my wife, Mrs. Yao, bought her,' indicating the maid's acquisition.
  • 才七八岁耳 - Means 'she was only seven or eight years old,' providing her age.
  • 卜问其父母安否 - Means 'to inquire about the safety of her parents,' describing the maid's concern.
  • 盖在婢妾中为有人心者 - Means 'she is one of the few with a human heart among maids and concubines,' praising her character.
  • 宜其有是福欤 - Means 'it is fitting that she has such good fortune,' expressing a wish or conclusion.
  • 或曰：“是中蛇毒也。” - Means 'someone said, 'This is due to snake poison,' attributing the sores to snake venom.
  • 募能捕之者 - Means 'they encouraged those who could catch it,' indicating a call for action.
  • 于城中得三丐 - Means 'they found three beggars in the city,' introducing the main characters.
  • 甲其师也，乙、丙皆其徒 - Means 'A is the master, B and C are his disciples,' establishing the hierarchy.
  • 索钱甚多，乡人醵与之 - Means 'they asked for a lot of money, which the villagers pooled together to give them,' describing the payment.
  • 既至，探得蛇窟 - Means 'after arriving, they discovered the snake's den,' indicating the discovery.
  • 甲命乙、丙分立左右 - Means 'A ordered B and C to stand on either side,' describing the setup.
  • 而自启其 - Means 'and opened his basket himself,' indicating the action.
  • 中贮蜈蚣无数 - Means 'which contained countless centipedes,' describing the contents.
  • 甲尽取食之 - Means 'A ate them all,' describing the action.
  • 须臾，自顶至踵皆肿 - Means 'soon, from head to toe, he was swollen,' describing the effect.
  • 甲闭目运气，久之其肿处皆消 - Means 'A closed his eyes and regulated his breath, and after a while, the swelling subsided,' describing the recovery.
  • 惟右手食指与中指则大几如股 - Means 'only his right index and middle fingers became as thick as thighs,' describing the remaining effect.
  • 即以此两指探入窟中 - Means 'he then used these two fingers to probe into the den,' describing the action.
  • 已而用力拔出 - Means 'and then with force, he pulled it out,' describing the extraction.
  • 乙、丙亦各曳其肘以助之 - Means 'B and C also pulled his elbows to help,' describing the assistance.
  • 指出，蛇随之出，则已毙矣 - Means 'as his fingers came out, the snake followed and was already dead,' describing the outcome.
  • 犹啮甲指不释 - Means 'it still bit A's fingers and wouldn't let go,' describing the snake's final act.
  • 乙、丙去其蛇，以药水洗之 - Means 'B and C removed the snake and washed it with medicinal water,' describing the cleanup.
  • 两指旋复故 - Means 'his two fingers soon returned to normal,' describing the recovery.
  • 蛇长丈许，粗倍人臂 - Means 'the snake was about a zhang long and twice as thick as a human arm,' describing the snake's size.
  • 其色黑暗如炭 - Means 'its color was dark as charcoal,' describing the snake's appearance.
  • 聚薪焚之，臭闻数里 - Means 'they gathered firewood and burned it, and the smell spread for several li,' describing the disposal.
  • 此三丐者，其技亦神矣 - Means 'these three beggars' skills were also divine,' praising their abilities.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 咸丰中 - Refers to the Xianfeng era (1850-1861) of the Qing dynasty.
  • 六合 - A place name, likely referring to Liuhe, a county in Jiangsu province.
  • 内子姚夫人 - Refers to the author's wife, Mrs. Yao, indicating a personal connection.
  • 蛇毒 - Refers to snake venom, a common cause of illness in traditional Chinese medicine.

Proper Nouns

  • 咸丰 - The reign title of Emperor Xianfeng of the Qing dynasty.
  • 六合 - A place name, likely referring to Liuhe, a county in Jiangsu province.
  • 姚夫人 - The name of the author's wife, Mrs. Yao.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 乾阿{弥女} - Affectionate or diminutive form of address for a young girl, possibly a relative.
  • 月事不行 - Euphemistic term for a woman’s menstrual cycle not occurring, often due to illness.
  • 雉经 - Method of suicide by hanging, derived from the practice of birds hanging themselves.
  • 物色 - Literal meaning 'to search for' or 'to look for,' but in context, refers to inquiring about or investigating.
  • 密室 - Hidden or secret room, often used for clandestine activities.
  • 白昼宣淫 - Literal meaning 'to openly engage in sexual activities during the day,' indicating immoral behavior.
  • 雉媒 - Individuals who act as intermediaries in arranging illicit romantic or sexual relationships.
  • 失身 - Literal meaning 'to lose one’s body,' but in context, refers to losing one’s chastity.
  • 自尽 - Neutral term for 'to take one’s own life,' less culturally specific than '雉经.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 沪上 - Refers to Shanghai, a major city known for its modernity and cosmopolitanism in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
  • 西洋人 - Refers to Westerners, particularly Europeans, associated with modern medicine and technology.
  • 东洋小车 - A small carriage, possibly a rickshaw, commonly used in urban areas during this time.
  • 菩提子 - Seeds from the bodhi tree, used in Buddhist practices such as prayer beads, suggesting a religious or spiritual context.
  • 密室 - Hidden or secret room, often used for clandestine activities like prostitution or other illicit activities.
  • 白昼宣淫 - Practice of engaging in sexual activities openly during the day, considered scandalous and immoral in traditional Chinese society.
  • 雉媒 - Individuals who act as intermediaries in arranging illicit romantic or sexual relationships, often involving young women.
  • 失身 - Concept of losing chastity, tied to the moral and social expectations placed on women in traditional Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

  • 西洋人 - General term for Westerners, particularly Europeans, associated with modern medicine and technology.
  • 东洋小车 - A type of small carriage, possibly a rickshaw, commonly used in urban areas during this time.
  • 菩提子 - Seeds from the bodhi tree, used in Buddhist practices.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 蘇城之初復也 - Classical phrase meaning 'At the beginning of the restoration of Suzhou,' referring to the city's recovery or reestablishment after a period of turmoil or destruction.
  • 孑然一身 - Common idiom meaning 'alone, without companions' or 'solitary and isolated.'
  • 恒寄宿于相识之家 - 'Often stayed as a guest in the homes of those he knew,' indicating a temporary and informal living arrangement.
  • 延之使教其子者 - 'Someone who invited him to tutor his son,' a common way to express employment or engagement in a teaching role.
  • 始至亦甚相得 - 'At first, they got along very well,' indicating a positive initial relationship.
  • 未及一月 - 'Before a month had passed,' indicating a short time period.
  • 屋舍狭小 - 'The house is small and cramped,' a direct description of the living space.
  • 虽书室亦须洒扫 - 'Even the study room must be cleaned,' indicating the need for preparation for guests.
  • 愿先生暂归一日 - 'I beg you, sir, to return for one day,' a polite request.
  • 实非所便 - 'It is not convenient for me,' expressing discomfort or inconvenience.
  • 庑下不辞 - 'I would not refuse even a place under the eaves,' indicating willingness to accept any available shelter.
  • 素来积贮薪蒸 - 'Has long been storing firewood and kindling,' indicating that the room had been used for storage.
  • 粪除 - 'To clean and prepare,' often used in the context of preparing a room for use.
  • 布席于彼 - 'To lay out a mat there,' indicating the act of making a space for someone to stay.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蘇城 - Refers to Suzhou, a historically significant city in Jiangsu Province, known for its cultural and economic prominence during the Ming and Qing dynasties. The reference to its 'restoration' suggests a context of war, rebellion, or disaster.
  • 貧士 - 'Poor scholar' or 'poor man of letters,' a common figure in classical Chinese literature, often representing moral integrity and perseverance despite hardship.
  • 避寇而歸 - 'Fled from bandits and returned home,' indicating a period of instability or conflict in the region.
  • 寄宿于相识之家 - The custom of staying with relatives or acquaintances was common in ancient China, especially for those without permanent housing.
  • 延之使教其子 - The practice of hiring private tutors for one's children was widespread among the literati class, especially during the imperial examination system era.
  • 書室 - 'Study room' or 'book room,' a space reserved for reading and writing, often considered a place of dignity and learning.
  • 庑下 - 'Under the eaves,' referring to the area outside a building but under its roof, often used as a place of temporary shelter.
  • 薪蒸 - 'Firewood and kindling,' indicating that the room was previously used for storage, possibly for heating or cooking.
  • 粪除 - A term used in classical Chinese to describe the process of cleaning and preparing a space for use, often associated with rituals or hospitality.

Proper Nouns

  • 蘇城 - Suzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province, China.
